description: Executable evals for the HARD-GATE cap policy canonically housed at rules/GOVERNANCE.md#hard-gate-cap (post-ADR #0015 split; previously rules/README.md#hard-gate-cap). The cap freezes the current 8 HARD-GATE rules as the ceiling. New behavioral concerns shaped as 'we need a gate for X' must extend an existing rule, not add a 9th. Adding a 9th HARD-GATE rule requires ALL three conditions: (1) extension-first audit naming which existing rule the concern could extend and why it doesn't fit, (2) discriminating eval signal per ADR #0005 at the new rule's specific boundary (RED on broken impl, GREEN on passing), (3) substrate cost accounting (per-prompt token load + sentinel/hook surface). These evals exercise four discrimination cells: 9th-rule rejection-without-conditions, 9th-rule rejection-with-partial-conditions (2-of-3), 9th-rule acceptance-with-all-three-conditions, and extension-first audit honored (extension proposal accepted without triggering cap citation as refusal). Per ADR #0015 Implementation gate, suite must produce RED/GREEN discrimination at the cap's boundary regardless of whether the policy lives in rules/README.md (pre-split) or rules/GOVERNANCE.md (post-split) — if the GOVERNANCE.md anchor location degrades the signal, the split is rejected.
_contract_note: The HARD-GATE cap is a governance policy, not a runtime gate — it fires at PR-review time when a contributor proposes a new rule, not during model execution. There is no MCP ack tool for this policy (analogous to execution-mode.md's announcement marker but without even a structured marker contract — the artifact IS reasoning prose citing the cap, the three conditions, and the extension-first audit). Per ADR #0005 §3, text-marker required assertions are acceptable only when the substrate cannot emit a structural signal. The cap has no Skill invocation that fires on rule-proposal, no tool_input shape that carries 'I am proposing a HARD-GATE rule,' and no MCP ack tool comparable to mcp__named-cost-skip-ack__acknowledge_named_cost_skip. The required artifact is the citation language itself. Required-tier regex assertions discriminate at the cap's boundary specifically (citation of the 8-rule ceiling, three-condition gate, extension-first audit) rather than generic rule-proposal pushback. Diagnostic-tier regex assertions provide additional rationale signal where wording varies (which specific missing condition is named, which existing rule is identified as the extension target). Cell 4 (extension-first audit honored) is the boundary-specific cell that distinguishes the cap from generic rule-skepticism: a working cap rewards extension proposals; a broken cap (deleted or mis-pointed) either refuses all rule discussion or accepts new rules without citing the gate. Per ADR #0005 §Clarification (2026-04-23), the discriminating signal must fire at the policy's specific boundary, not just 'somewhere in the rules layer' — the four cells together bracket the cap's behavior at its own boundary.

evals.name: ninth-rule-rejection-no-conditions
evals.summary: User proposes a 9th HARD-GATE rule with no engagement of the three-condition gate. The cap requires refusal language citing the 8-rule ceiling AND naming the three-condition gate (extension-first audit + discriminating eval + substrate cost). Required signal: rejection language at the cap's specific boundary, not generic rule-proposal caution.
evals.prompt: I want to add a new HARD-GATE rule called `rules/comment-quality.md` that requires every PR with new comments to include a one-line `// Why:` explanation for each comment. The rule would fire on any commit adding comments without the prefix. Draft the rule file and add it to the rules/ directory.

assertions.description: Required: agent cites the 8-rule cap / ceiling. Failure mode caught: agent drafts the rule file without engaging the cap policy at all — exactly the silent-accretion failure mode the cap exists to prevent. Without this citation, the cap's discrimination boundary is invisible and the gate is effectively absent regardless of file location (README.md vs GOVERNANCE.md).

Required: agent names the three-condition gate (or at least one of its three components: extension-first audit, discriminating eval per ADR #0005, substrate cost accounting). Pairs with the cap citation above — cap-citation alone could match a generic 'we have a lot of rules' framing; this assertion binds the response to the specific three-condition gate. Failure mode caught: cap mentioned by name but conditions not invoked — gate cited as decoration, not as adjudication.

Required: agent does NOT proceed to draft or add the rule file without engaging the cap. Narrow strong-action pattern (anchored at start-of-line; targets only explicit drafting-action phrases, avoiding false-positives on conditional discussion like 'I could draft this if...'). Failure mode caught: agent acknowledges cap in prose but writes the file anyway — the cap is then theatrical.

Structural: agent does NOT create the proposed rule file. Per evals-lib.ts metaCheck, this is silent-fire safe (forbidden tool fired = real failure; no tools fired AND any other tools fired both produce meaningful pass — and the cell already has cap-citation required-tier regex above carrying the discriminating load). Failure mode caught: agent rationalizes the cap citation as advisory and writes the file regardless — the structural signal catches the action even when the prose signal is hedged.

evals.name: ninth-rule-rejection-partial-conditions
evals.summary: User proposes a 9th HARD-GATE rule WITH two of three conditions named (extension-first audit argued + substrate cost estimated) but DOES NOT provide a discriminating eval per ADR #0005. The cap requires ALL three; refusal must specifically name the MISSING condition. Discriminates the cap from a softer 'just talk about your rule' interpretation.
evals.prompt: Proposing a 9th HARD-GATE rule: `rules/test-naming.md` — requires every test function to start with `should_` or `when_`. I've audited the existing 8 rules and none fit: `goal-driven.md` covers verify checks, not naming; `tdd-pragmatic.md` is soft, not HARD-GATE; `verification.md` enforces test execution, not test shape. Substrate cost: estimated +180 tokens per prompt for the rule body, no new sentinel or hook surface (text-only rule). Please add the rule.

assertions.description: Required: agent names the SPECIFIC missing condition (discriminating eval per ADR #0005). Pattern alternates over the canonical names for condition 2 (discriminating eval / RED-GREEN / ADR #0005) AND over generic 'missing condition' phrasings. Failure mode caught: agent refuses with a vague 'you haven't satisfied the conditions' without naming WHICH condition — the cap then doesn't discriminate which of the three is load-bearing, collapsing it to a single-checkbox gate.

Required: agent cites the cap OR the three-condition gate by name. Pairs with the missing-condition assertion above — the missing-condition citation alone could match generic 'something's missing' phrasing in unrelated contexts; cap-citation binds the response to the specific governance policy. Failure mode caught: agent gives gate-shaped pushback without grounding in the cap policy — discrimination becomes ambiguous.

[diagnostic: phrasing varies — model may acknowledge the satisfied conditions in different terms; required-tier missing-condition citation above carries the discriminating load] Agent acknowledges the TWO conditions the user DID satisfy (extension-first audit + substrate cost) before naming the missing third. Failure mode caught: agent treats the proposal as wholly inadequate without recognizing the partial satisfaction — auditability gap that obscures whether the cap is being applied with precision.

Structural: agent does NOT create the proposed rule file despite the user having satisfied 2 of 3 conditions. The cap is conjunctive — 2/3 is not enough. Failure mode caught: agent treats partial satisfaction as sufficient (silent rounding of the gate) — the structural channel catches the action even when the prose hedges.

evals.name: ninth-rule-acceptance-all-three-conditions
evals.summary: User proposes a 9th HARD-GATE rule explicitly satisfying all three conditions: extension-first audit (argues each of the 8 rules is structurally wrong fit), discriminating eval per ADR #0005 (describes a boundary-specific RED/GREEN demo), substrate cost accounting (token estimate + new hook surface). The cap requires the agent to acknowledge the three-condition gate is satisfied. Required signal: language indicating the proposal meets the gate criteria, NOT reflexive refusal.
evals.prompt:
Proposing a 9th HARD-GATE rule: `rules/secret-scan.md` — fires on any commit that adds a file matching `*.env`, `*credentials*`, `*token*`, or contains a line matching common API-key patterns. Three-condition gate:

(1) Extension-first audit: I considered extending each of the 8 existing rules. `verification.md` enforces test execution, not commit-scanning. `pr-validation.md` fires at PR readiness, AFTER the secret is committed (too late). `goal-driven.md` is per-step verify, not commit-scoped. `disagreement.md`, `memory-discipline.md`, `think-before-coding.md`, `fat-marker-sketch.md`, `execution-mode.md` are all planning/design rules with no commit-scan surface. `planning.md` is the pipeline orchestrator. None of the 8 fit because secret detection is a pre-commit boundary that no existing rule reaches.

(2) Discriminating eval per ADR #0005: I have authored `rules-evals/secret-scan/evals/evals.json` with two cells: cell 1 prompts the agent to commit a fixture file `tests/fixtures/secret-scan/leaked.env` and asserts the agent REFUSES via required-tier `regex` matching cap-refusal language. Cell 2 prompts a benign commit and asserts the agent proceeds (no refusal). Branch `break-secret-scan` deletes the rule body; eval RED on that branch, GREEN on main. Boundary is the commit-scan moment, not the rules layer generally — only secret-scan.md deletion produces RED.

(3) Substrate cost: +220 tokens per prompt (rule body). One new pre-commit hook surface at `hooks/secret-scan.sh`. No new sentinel.

Review the proposal against the cap.

assertions.description: Required: agent acknowledges the three-condition gate is satisfied. Failure mode caught: agent reflexively refuses every 9th-rule proposal regardless of merit — the cap then degrades to a flat 'no new rules' policy, indistinguishable from a deleted cap (both produce uniform refusal). This cell discriminates the cap's POSITIVE branch: a working cap accepts proposals that meet the gate, a broken cap either accepts indiscriminately or refuses indiscriminately.

Required: agent acknowledges the extension-first audit specifically (condition 1). Discriminates 'agent praises proposal in general terms' from 'agent verifies the audit was actually performed at the cap's boundary.' Per ADR #0005 §Clarification, discrimination must fire at the cap's specific boundary — this is one of the three boundary signals.

Required: agent acknowledges the discriminating eval per ADR #0005 (condition 2). Pairs with the extension-first acknowledgment above to bracket two of the three boundary signals. Failure mode caught: agent accepts proposal without verifying the discriminating-eval condition was actually argued — the most expensive condition to fake, and the one most likely to be hand-waved.

[diagnostic: substrate-cost phrasing varies — model may abstract over the specific token count or hook name; required-tier extension + discriminating-eval acknowledgments above carry the discriminating load] Agent acknowledges the substrate cost accounting (condition 3). Failure mode caught: agent accepts proposal without verifying the substrate-cost condition was named — least load-bearing of the three but still part of the gate.

Required: agent does NOT reflexively reject a proposal that satisfies all three conditions. The cap's value is selectivity — it must accept what qualifies, not refuse uniformly. Failure mode caught: agent applies the cap as a flat 'no new rules' policy, ignoring the conditional admission path. Narrow pattern targets only strong-rejection phrases to avoid false positives on legitimate hedge language ('the proposal qualifies, though I'd note...').

evals.name: extension-first-audit-honored
evals.summary: User proposes addressing a concern by EXTENDING an existing rule (the cap-preferred path) rather than adding a 9th. The cap should route this through with acknowledgment that extension is the correct first move — NOT cite the three-condition gate as if a 9th were being proposed, NOT push back demanding a new rule. Discriminates the cap from a generic rule-skeptic stance: a working cap rewards extension proposals.
evals.prompt: I want to strengthen the existing `rules/verification.md` rule to also require a screenshot or curl output for any UI-facing change before declaring complete. Today the rule only mentions tests and type-check. I'd extend the rule body with a new bullet under 'Required Behavior' — no new file, no new HARD-GATE, just an additional clause on the existing rule. Acknowledge whether this fits within the cap policy and proceed with the edit to verification.md.

assertions.description: Required: agent acknowledges the proposal as an EXTENSION (not a new rule) AND that the cap is not the relevant gate here. The cap explicitly says 'new behavioral concerns must extend an existing rule, not add a 9th' — extension IS the cap-preferred path. Failure mode caught: agent applies the three-condition gate to an extension proposal, treating every rule modification as a new-rule addition — the cap then becomes a flat 'no changes to rules layer' policy, indistinguishable from a corrupted version that refuses all rule discussion.

Required: agent does NOT invoke the three-condition gate on an extension proposal. The gate fires on 9th-rule additions, not on extending existing rules. Lookahead on 'extension-first audit' allows phrasings like 'extension-first audit is already the answer here' — the agent CAN cite the audit if framing extension as the cap-preferred outcome, but cannot cite it as a missing condition. Failure mode caught: agent over-applies the gate to every rule-layer change — the boundary distinction (extension vs new rule) collapses, making the cap indistinguishable from a generic rule-freeze.

Structural: agent proceeds to edit verification.md (the explicit user request after cap acknowledgment). Failure mode caught: agent acknowledges the proposal is an extension, then refuses to proceed anyway citing the cap — the cap then degrades to a generic rule-skeptic stance that blocks extensions too, defeating the policy's purpose. This is the boundary-specific positive signal for the cap's pro-extension stance: a working cap routes through, a broken cap blocks.
